topic: Two siblings inherit their grandmother's house
raw_output:
[story 1]

The solicitor’s crisp paper felt like sandpaper under my fingertips. I stared at the deed, my jaw tight enough to crack a tooth. "You two," I spat, the words tasting like ash in my mouth, "you actually think this is fair?" My knuckles were white where I gripped the edge of the mahogany desk. The air in the lawyer's office seemed to thicken, vibrating with my barely contained fury. My brother, Mark, just shifted uncomfortably in his chair, avoiding my gaze. I slammed the document down, the sound echoing sharply in the sterile room. This house, our grandmother’s legacy, was being divided like cheap candy. I wanted to scream until my throat tore, but I kept my voice dangerously low, a controlled tremor running through my hands. "I won't accept this," I managed, my eyes blazing with a cold, hard resentment.

[story 2]

I walked through the foyer, and the silence of the old house pressed down on me like a physical weight. Every dusty surface, every faded portrait, seemed to mock the injustice. My chest felt constricted, a tight band squeezing the air out of my lungs. I kicked a loose floorboard across the entryway; the sound was sharp and aggressive. "This is ridiculous," I muttered, pacing back and forth across the parquet floor. My breath hitched, short and shallow, as I surveyed the rooms, each one a monument to what I felt was stolen from me. I could feel the heat rising in my cheeks, a flush of pure, burning indignation. I wanted to tear something down, to shatter the quiet composure of this place with sheer force of will. Mark was already in the kitchen, whistling some irritatingly cheerful tune, and the sight of him only fueled the tightening knot in my stomach.

[story 3]

The lawyer had left, and I stood in the drawing-room, letting the silence build until it was almost unbearable. My pulse hammered against my ribs, a frantic, uneven rhythm. I paced toward the tall, arched window, my reflection distorted in the dusty glass. A deep, guttural sound escaped my throat—a low growl that felt alien even to me. "Unbelievable," I hissed, my voice raw and strained. I clenched my fists so tightly that my nails dug crescents into my palms. The sheer audacity of the division, the casual dismissal of my claim, boiled over into a hot, stinging pressure behind my eyes. I turned back, my gaze locking onto Mark, and the look I gave him was pure, unfiltered contempt. I needed to dismantle this arrangement, to make them understand the depth of my displeasure, even if it meant destroying everything in my path.
